skill.name: base
skill.description: Create, connect, and automate LibreOffice Base databases, forms, reports, and data workflows.
skill.summary: Guide LibreOffice Base database creation, connections, forms, reports, and automation.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static Ruby backtick and weak cryptography findings are false positives caused by Markdown fences and the word Base. The skill is not malicious, but it includes database password handling, file URL writes, local automation sockets, and process-killing commands that require user caution.

medium_findings.title: Database Password Stored In Example Data Source
medium_findings.description: The example assigns a password into LibreOffice data source properties. This is legitimate database automation, but generated ODB files may retain connection credentials.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly assigns a password property and stores a connected ODB file. It is framed as an example, so the risk is contextual rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Local UNO Automation Socket Guidance
medium_findings.description: The troubleshooting command starts LibreOffice with a local UNO socket listener. This is normal for automation, but users should avoid exposing it beyond localhost.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The listener is explicitly bound to localhost and used for LibreOffice automation. The risk is real if users adapt the command to a wider network interface.

medium_findings.title: Process Termination Command In Troubleshooting
medium_findings.description: The guide recommends killing LibreOffice processes during socket troubleshooting. This can cause unsaved work loss if run without checking active documents.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command explicitly terminates soffice.bin processes. The behavior is clear, but it appears intended as local troubleshooting guidance.

low_findings.title: External Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The resources section links to LibreOffice, UNO, HSQLDB, and Firebird documentation. These are expected references, not evidence of data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are plain documentation links in a resources section. No code sends user data or credentials to these sites.

low_findings.title: Markdown Fence Static-Analysis False Positives
low_findings.description: Several Ruby backtick execution alerts point to Markdown code fences, not executable Ruby syntax. The fenced examples are documentation content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected lines are Markdown fences and example blocks. No Ruby file or runtime backtick expression is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static-Analysis False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts correspond to Base-related prose and headings. No hashing, encryption, or cryptographic algorithm usage is shown.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Those lines discuss LibreOffice Base capabilities, forms, reports, and query design. No cryptographic primitive is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Assignment Before File Save
dangerous_patterns.description: The example sets a password property and then stores the connected database document. Users should avoid embedding production credentials in ODB files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The sequence is explicit in the sample code. The skill does not exfiltrate credentials, but the storage pattern can create local secret exposure.

dangerous_patterns.title: Process Kill Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The troubleshooting section includes killall for LibreOffice. This can interrupt unrelated LibreOffice sessions on the same machine.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command and process name are visible. The operational impact is clear even though the intent is troubleshooting.

content.value_statement: Database setup in LibreOffice Base can be slow when tables, forms, reports, and connections are planned separately. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through practical Base workflows.
actual_capabilities: Guides creation of LibreOffice Base ODB databases.
Explains table, view, relationship, and query planning.
Shows how to connect Base to MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQLite, JDBC, and ODBC sources.
Supports form and report design workflows.
Provides command-line and Python UNO automation examples.
Lists troubleshooting steps for local LibreOffice automation sockets.
limitations: Requires LibreOffice and any needed database drivers to be installed.
Does not include a runnable script package or test suite.
Connection examples use placeholders that users must replace safely.
Does not validate live database credentials or schemas.

use_cases.title: Create a Small Office Database
use_cases.description: Plan an ODB file with tables, relationships, forms, and reports for everyday records.

use_cases.title: Connect Base to Existing Data
use_cases.description: Prepare connection strings and driver requirements for MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQLite, JDBC, or ODBC sources.

use_cases.title: Automate Report Generation
use_cases.description: Use LibreOffice headless mode and UNO concepts to design repeatable database reporting workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a New Base Database
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a LibreOffice Base database for my records. Include tables, fields, relationships, forms, and reports.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare an External Connection
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a safe LibreOffice Base connection plan for my database type, host, port, database name, and driver requirements.

prompt_templates.title: Design Forms and Reports
prompt_templates.prompt: Turn this database schema into a Base form and report plan with field groups, filters, and summary sections.

prompt_templates.title: Review an Automation Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this LibreOffice Base automation workflow for reliability, credential handling, file paths, and local socket safety.

output_examples.input: I need a customer tracking database for a small service business.
output_examples.output: A suggested ODB design with customer, job, invoice, and payment tables, plus entry forms and monthly reports.

output_examples.input: I want to connect LibreOffice Base to PostgreSQL.
output_examples.output: A connection checklist covering driver setup, host and port values, database name, authentication handling, and validation steps.

output_examples.input: My headless Base automation cannot connect.
output_examples.output: A troubleshooting path for checking LibreOffice processes, local socket settings, driver installation, and connection string format.

best_practices: Use parameterized queries and least-privilege database accounts.
Back up ODB files before schema changes or automation runs.
Keep production credentials out of shared ODB files and prompts.
anti_patterns: Do not paste production passwords into reusable prompts.
Do not expose LibreOffice automation sockets to untrusted networks.
Do not run process-killing commands before checking active documents.

faq.question: What does this skill help me build?
faq.answer: It helps plan and automate LibreOffice Base databases, forms, reports, queries, and external database connections.

faq.question: Does it create a complete database automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples that users adapt in LibreOffice and their local environment.

faq.question: Which database systems are covered?
faq.answer: It covers embedded HSQLDB and Firebird, plus MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQLite, JDBC, and ODBC connection concepts.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude Code or Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is marked for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.

faq.question: Are there security concerns?
faq.answer: Yes. Review credential handling, local socket usage, file paths, and process termination commands before use.

faq.question: Does it include official LibreOffice documentation links?
faq.answer: Yes. It links to LibreOffice, UNO API, HSQLDB, and Firebird documentation resources.
